[14:28] stuff and all of them are really about

[14:36] to most functional providers is finding out that ckd is so
[14:42] prevalent it affects over 37 million Americans
[14:46] and the number is rising why is the number rising the number is
[14:50] rising because insulin resistance is rampant
[14:52] we're epidemically uh struggling with 90 percent of the
[14:57] population give or take depending on which data you read
[15:00] with insulin resistance diabetes PCOS um hormone imbalances all of that is
[15:06] impacted by insulin resistance and so all of that turns into
[15:09] chronic kidney disease over time and so that's the leading cause of course of
[15:14] dialysis and or transplant from our perspective we know that
[15:19] chronic kidney disease didn't develop last week
[15:22] or even last month chronic kidney disease develops over many months
[15:27] many years the same way insulin resistance
[15:30] precedes diabetes type two it takes years
[15:34] and decades often for ckd to become a parent
[15:39] and as you see here it's often silence silent until its advanced stages
[15:46] most of us are familiar with mainstream causes of
[15:49] ckd we all are familiar with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
[15:54] those are the two big things out there that we know
[15:57] cause chronic kidney disease many people are unaware of how impactful
[16:03] obesity and insulin resistance are to kidney
[16:06] health but they they do damage many autoimmune diseases and or the
[16:11] medications for the autoimmune diseases will also contribute to ckd kidney stones
[16:16] will contribute as well if they're chronic
[16:19] and not well managed smoking has always been associated with kidney
[16:23] disease and then of course we know medications are
[16:25] nephrotoxic in many cases many of us forget to look
[16:29] that up so i really encourage you as you become
[16:32] more functionally minded that you look at medications
[16:36] if there is a renal or hepatic dose adjustment that means that you're
[16:42] causing either renal or liver damage so look at that
[16:46] when you look your medicines up why does it matter
[16:52] if insulin resistance is part of the problem
[16:55] well insulin resistance
[16:59] is this big kind of balloon so if you will imagine this big
[17:07] vin diagram and insulin resistance is in the middle
[17:10] and then all around it you have these overlapping pieces like
[17:15] hyperfiltration in the glomerular function lots of salt being retained
[17:22] remember when insulin goes up sodium begins to fall and so then
[17:28] by nature the body says oh we're losing sodium let me keep some
[17:33] and so it triggers sodium retention and so that results in swelling it results in
[17:38] albumin functions that are sort of kind of
[17:43] displaced or become problematic patients have higher and
[17:50] higher blood pressure of course sometimes they go the other
[17:53] way we wind up with hypo tension instead of hyper sometimes
[17:57] because of poor water poor salt intake all of these things will also
[18:01] contribute to damage to the inside of arteries and
[18:05] blood vessels and so then you have oxidative stress
[18:08] and that includes some chronic inflammation so that
[18:12] results in progressive kidney injury so it's a downward spiral and this is
[18:17] just another way to look at it
[18:21] so our functional recommendation number one is reverse the insulin resistance it's
[18:26] easy to reverse it in theory it's harder to convince patients to do the things
[18:32] to reverse insulin resistance but look at their specific medications if
[18:37] they are on meds for type 2 diabetes for
[18:42] instance let's say they're on some glipizide and metformin
[18:46] both of those are nephrotoxic maybe reduce their doses
[18:49] or get them off of metformin and replace that with berberine
[18:53] they work in the same pathways but berberine is less toxic
[18:57] so there are always things you can do to move medications around
[19:04] yes your aces and arbs are considered kidney supportive
[19:09] but they're also nephrotoxic so depending on the stage of their kidney
[19:14] disease you want to be careful putting them on
[19:18] aces or arps so just be aware look it up look at the
[19:22] creatinine clearance look at the dose adjustment that may be needed
[19:27] sometimes we do even in functional care sometimes i will put patients on
[19:32] losartan or lisinopril for kidney protection if their kidneys aren't
[19:37] looking great and they're not a hundred percent on
[19:40] board with functional stuff you have to kind of be a hybrid person
[19:45] sometimes you have to do functional stuff but you also have to include
[19:48] mainstream stuff some of the time so depending on each situation
[19:52] you may want to use medications as well as functional recommendations
[19:57] get patients to reduce or eliminate their grains refined carbs
[20:02] that processed food is actually the worst thing they can do for their
[20:05] kidneys getting rid of beverages like sugary stuff
[20:11] sodas carbonation energy drinks
[20:16] even tea sugary tea is dangerous to kidneys
[20:21] so make sure patients are aware of what things are safer
[20:25] for their kidneys i have a ckd dot phrase that
[20:29] i put in my patient's chart when they start having
[20:32] changes to their labs that indicate some mild kidney problems

[31:20] keep in mind that metformin and berberine work very similarly
[31:25] i've never seen lactic acid build up from berberine but because of how they
[31:29] work that is a possibility because of that
[31:31] possibility i don't put people on metformin and berberine there was a
[31:36] small study uh a few years ago that showed it was
[31:40] fine to use them both but i i mean to me it sounds like
[31:46] you know overdosing of medicine you're using the same thing
[31:50] in two different ways or two different products
[31:53] and so we we don't typically do that in medicine
[31:56] so i'm not i'm not a fan of using them together

[32:04] with glp ones um but i don't use it with berberine you can use inositol with

[58:00] why does sleep matter sleep matters for a whole bunch of reasons
[58:05] um
[58:08] making sure i got all the comments uh in the chat
[58:15] um uh uh sleep matters because cortisol regulation is
[58:20] closely tied to insulin resistance immune dysfunction cognition hormones weight
[58:27] management cardiovascular health brain function like head to toe every
[58:30] single thing your body has to get sleep and so i
[58:34] am a huge advocate for apple watches fit bits
[58:38] aura rings anything that you can use any of the little devices that will help you
[58:44] understand sleep patterns better um uh so keep that in mind uh poor
[58:51] sleep can mimic or be caused by hypothyroidism
[58:56] hyperthyroidism anxiety depression attention deficit problems
[59:02] chronic fatigue why are they chronically tired
[59:05] because they're not sleeping it's just this vicious cycle like i told you all
[59:10] about earlier and then of course um estrogen dominance or testosterone
[59:15] imbalances both of those drive energy if those are high-ish then
[59:20] bodies can't come down when it's time to go to bed
[59:24] because that drives progesterone low when estrogen and testosterone are high
[59:28] so we prescribe just uh progesterone those levels start to fall and
[59:32] patients go to sleep and they they feel better
[59:36] so this is a screen that is real helpful uh it kind of
[59:40] puts most of your optimal lab values in one little
[59:43] graphic for you um and helps uh with your learning
[59:48] what values you're aiming for and so this can be a real helpful graphic for
[59:52] y'all before you just tell a patient oh
[59:56] you're having trouble sleeping take melatonin i don't ever use melatonin
[60:01] um but don't just willy-nilly recommend something for sleep
[60:07] sleep doesn't get disrupted just because
[60:10] sleep is disrupted for various reasons um it's
[60:14] important that we understand nutrition plays a huge role in
[60:18] cortisol and circadian rhythm so does insulin management weight uh gain will
[60:25] play a huge role in poor sleep because the body
[60:29] habitus is now struggling with the excess weight
[60:33] on that body positions are more uncomfortable
[60:37] hips hurt knees hurt backs hurt um trying to find a comfortable position
[60:44] requires often multiple pillows or supportive agents
[60:48] to help patients get in a comfortable supported position
[60:51] so they can actually rest um and so think about all of those things
[60:56] when you're looking at reasons that sleep is
[60:59] disruptive is the patient suffering pain if you've got a
[61:04] perimenopausal menopausal woman and she tells you she's got hip pain every night
[61:08] when she's sleeping she's estrogen problematic
[61:11] right there like you've already found part of the problem
[61:13] um estrogen deficits almost always contribute to sleep hip pain
[61:19] it's typically referred to as luteal tendinopathy
[61:22] very common in your perimenopausal low estrogen
[61:25] level women menopausal women if your patients have had
[61:29] surgical hysterectomies and have surgical menopause
[61:34] they're almost always going to report hip pain at some point
[61:38] um and so you want to address that and help them get better estrogen levels
[61:43] alcohol use will drive a lot of problems of sleep even though it is a
[61:48] depressant um and helps bring things down some
[61:51] people will sometimes self-medicate sleep patterns with alcohol because it
[61:56] does help people fall asleep the problem is it doesn't last and so
[61:59] then they have tossing and turning all night long and they don't rest
[62:03] so they wake up in the morning not well rested
[62:07] and they don't feel well consider various questionnaires thyroid
[62:10] questionnaires clinical profiles that assess a wide
[62:14] variety of systems um use the stop bang to assess for
[62:18] sleep apnea use your wearables look at hormone levels
[62:22] cgm data will sometimes be really helpful if you can convince patients to
[62:26] use one because if their glucose is dropping
[62:29] in the middle of the night that will cause sleep disruptions as well
[62:40] um
[62:43] looking to see what over here um in the chat um
[62:52] sleep hygiene i'm not going to go into this a lot but it really plays a huge
[62:56] role um if people are on their phones watching
[63:00] tv's playing on their computers until late at night the blue light from the
[63:05] screens are going to trigger rods and
[63:08] cones in the eyeball and be very
[63:12] stimulating and so that will create a lot of
[63:15] problems with bedtime and wake time and falling
[63:18] asleep staying asleep um uh data shows us that the perfect
[63:23] temperature for sleep is about 65 degrees
[63:26] winter or summer and so keeping your rooms very cool at night allows for
[63:31] people to cover up and to be cuddly under covers
[63:35] um uh and comfortable um so keep that in mind some people can't
[63:40] tolerate it quite that cool but the data does show 65 is your best
[63:45] temperature for sleeping so if people talk about needing a fan ask
[63:49] what their thermometer their thermostat is set on for their home air
[63:53] conditioning or heating system because if it's too warm the body will not
[63:58] settle down and will not sleep um uh think about all the things you're
[64:03] eating drinking all day long address some of those things
[64:07] reading listening to books listening to scripture
[64:11] listening to poems um all those kinds of things can help
[64:15] bring things down a little bit look for those overnight glucose swings when
[64:20] you're using a cgm somewhere between two and four is often related to poor
[64:25] glucose management it's also associated with poor progesterone
[64:30] um and so if women start telling me they are waking every night at three a.m my

[64:41] progesterone 200 milligrams p ohs and we'll see how they do for a while
[64:45] patients that are reporting night sweats palpitations vivid dreams
[64:49] um hot flashes all that kind of stuff is definitely driven by hormone
[64:53] imbalances and that needs to be addressed

[64:59] that have lots of refined carbohydrates or sugars at

[65:20] it and so helping patients understand that
